title: Recognizing the history of the drug diethylstilbestrol and the harm it has caused, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This resolution asks Congress to recognize the harm caused by a drug called DES that doctors gave to millions of pregnant women from the 1940s to 1971. The drug was supposed to prevent pregnancy problems, but it actually caused cancer and other serious health issues in the children of women who took it. The resolution wants the FDA to apologize to the families who were hurt by this drug and to support creating a special week each year to raise awareness about DES and its dangers.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Recognizing the history of the drug diethylstilbestrol and the harm it has caused, and for other purposes
sections.text: This resolution talks about a harmful drug called diethylstilbestrol or DES. DES is a dangerous artificial hormone that can cause cancer and disrupt the body's natural hormone system. The Food and Drug Administration approved this drug in the early nineteen forties. Doctors gave DES to between five and ten million pregnant women in the United States from the early nineteen forties until nineteen seventy one. They thought it would help prevent miscarriages. However, when babies were exposed to DES before birth, many female children later developed rare cancers of the vagina and cervix called clear cell adenocarcinoma. These affected daughters are often called DES Daughters. The FDA banned the use of DES for pregnant women in nineteen seventy one. Since then, scientists have learned that DES exposure before birth also causes fertility problems, pregnancy complications, and other physical and mental health issues. The FDA has admitted that DES caused harm but has not apologized to the families who were hurt by its long use. The resolution suggests that April twentieth through twenty sixth, two thousand twenty five, should be called DES Awareness Week. The House of Representatives supports creating DES Awareness Week, recognizes the history and harm of DES, supports bringing back funding for the DES Follow Up Study, and asks the FDA to give a formal apology to families affected by DES.
